title: How Ansible Connects to Hosts
description:
## Agent or No Agent?

A team is choosing a configuration management tool and wants to avoid installing and maintaining a long-running daemon on every server. They're considering **Ansible**. Which statement correctly describes how Ansible operates?

options.text: Ansible is **agentless** — it pushes configuration to hosts over SSH, driven by YAML playbooks, an inventory of hosts, and reusable modules. No daemon is required on the managed nodes.
Ansible requires a persistent agent daemon installed and running on every managed node, which polls a central server for changes.
Ansible only works on the control machine and cannot configure remote hosts at all.
Ansible replaces SSH with its own custom always-on network protocol that must be opened on every host.

explanation: Ansible is **agentless**: it connects to managed hosts over standard **SSH** (WinRM on Windows) and **pushes** changes from a control node, so there's nothing to install or keep running on the targets beyond Python. You describe desired state in **YAML playbooks**, list targets in an **inventory**, and use **modules** as the units of work. This contrasts with the classic **Chef/Puppet** model (option b), where an **agent daemon** on each node periodically **pulls** configuration from a central server. Option (c) is wrong — configuring remote hosts is exactly Ansible's purpose. Option (d) is wrong — Ansible uses ordinary SSH, not a bespoke always-on protocol.

hints.title: Push over SSH vs pull with an agent
hints.body: Ansible = agentless, push over SSH. Chef/Puppet = an agent daemon on each node that pulls from a server. The team wants to avoid daemons, so think agentless.
